## Why use species distribution models for Blue 5? {background-color="white" .technical-white .sdm-context}

<div class="sb5-header">IKI - Save the Blue 5 (SB5)</div>
<h2 class="slide-title">Why use species distribution models for Blue 5?</h2>

Species distribution models are useful here because they integrate species information with physical and biogeochemical ocean conditions to estimate where suitable habitat occurs now, and how that suitability may change under climate change.

<div class="sdm-bridge-flow">
<div class="sdm-inputs">
<div class="sdm-node">Species records</div>
<div class="sdm-plus">+</div>
<div class="sdm-node">Ocean conditions</div>
</div>
<div class="sdm-arrow">→</div>
<div class="sdm-node sdm-output">Habitat suitability</div>
<div class="sdm-arrow">→</div>
<div class="sdm-node sdm-impact">Climate change impacts</div>
</div>

- Species distribution models link species occurrence records with the ocean conditions that shape habitat suitability.
- They allow us to translate physical climate change into potential biological responses.
- For Blue 5, this helps identify where suitable habitats may persist, decline, or shift in the future.

---

## Our baseline species distribution model framework {background-color="white" .technical-white .sdm-framework}

<div class="sb5-header">IKI - Save the Blue 5 (SB5)</div>
<h2 class="slide-title">Our baseline species distribution model framework</h2>

We use an ensemble species distribution modeling approach that combines multiple algorithms, from classical statistical models to machine-learning methods.

- Ensemble of 10 algorithms, including GLMs, GAMs, MaxEnt, Random Forest, and Boosted Regression Trees.

<div class="sdm-equation">

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{logit}(p_i) =
&\alpha +
\beta_T T_i +
\beta_S S_i +
\beta_O O_{2i} +
\beta_{Chl} Chl_i +
\beta_V V_i \\
&+
\beta_F FrontFreq_i +
\beta_U Upw_i +
\beta_E ENSO_t +
\varepsilon_i
\end{aligned}
$$

</div>

<div class="sdm-predictor-groups">
<div>
<strong>State variables</strong>
<p>Temperature, salinity, dissolved oxygen, chlorophyll, and current velocity.</p>
</div>
<div>
<strong>Ocean variability predictors</strong>
<p>Ocean front frequency, upwelling, and ENSO.</p>
</div>
</div>

<div class="sdm-note">
Conceptual representation of the predictors used to estimate probability of occurrence or habitat suitability. This is not the exact fitted formula for every algorithm in the ensemble.
</div>
